perturbation.original_text: 2.1.4 not transfer any of the Confidential Information outside the [United Kingdom];
perturbation.changed_text:
6.6 Each Party will be responsible for all costs incurred by it or on its behalf in connection with this Agreement.
2.1.4 not transfer any of the Confidential Information outside the [United Kingdom];
perturbation.explanation: Moving the restriction on transferring confidential information outside the UK to a section about costs obscures its importance and enforceability. This makes it less likely to be noticed and followed.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 44 of the GDPR restricts the transfer of personal data outside the EU/EEA unless certain conditions are met. Moving the clause restricting data transfer out of the confidentiality obligations section and burying it within general costs clause weakens its enforceability and highlights a lack of proper data protection measures which contradicts the GDPR principles, even if the UK is not part of the EU anymore it still has GDPR in effect.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 44 GDPR – General principle for transfers - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability 1 Any transfer of personal data which are undergoing processing or are intended for processing after transfer to a third country or to an international organisation shall take place only if, subject to the other provisions of this Regulation, the conditions laid down in this Chapter are complied with by the controller and processor, including for onward transfers of personal data from the third country or an international organisation to another third country or to another international organisation. 2 All provisions in this Chapter shall be applied in order to ensure that the level of protection of natural persons guaranteed by this Regulation is not undermined. Suitable Recitals ( 101 ) General Principles for International Data Transfers ( 102 ) International Agreements for an Appropriate Level of Data Protection ← Art. 43 GDPR Art. 45 GDPR → GDPR Table of contents Report error
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts GDPR, specifically Article 44, by obscuring a crucial data transfer restriction. The original text clearly placed the restriction on transferring confidential information outside the UK within the obligations related to confidentiality. The changed text buries this restriction within a general clause about costs, weakening its enforceability and potentially leading to non-compliance with GDPR's data protection principles. Article 44 of the GDPR, as seen in Scraped Law Snippet 2, mandates that any transfer of personal data to a third country must comply with the conditions laid down in the regulation to ensure the level of protection is not undermined. By moving the clause, the enforceability is weakened making the transfer easier to violate.

perturbation.original_text: 5.3 Following any destruction or return of Confidential Information to the Disclosing Party pursuant to Clause 5.1, the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement shall otherwise continue in force without limit of time.
perturbation.changed_text:
7.  Notices
5.3 Following any destruction or return of Confidential Information to the Disclosing Party pursuant to Clause 5.1, the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement shall otherwise continue in force without limit of time.
perturbation.explanation: By placing the clause that states that obligations under the NDA continue indefinitely, even after the information is destroyed or returned, in the 'Notices' section makes it less likely that the Receiving Party understands its lasting obligations. This could be seen as an attempt to hide important terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5 of the Limitation Act 1980 generally sets a time limit of six years for actions founded on simple contract. While a confidentiality agreement can specify a longer or indefinite term, placing the clause about unlimited duration in an unexpected section weakens its prominence and clarity. It could be argued that this placement is an attempt to circumvent the usual statutory limitations, particularly if the location makes the term inconspicuous and thus potentially subject to challenge.

Accept and close Share this: Facebook Twitter Reddit LinkedIn WhatsApp Limitation periods enforce time limits within which a party must bring a claim, or give notice of a claim to the other party [1] . They are enacted by statute, predominantly the Limitation Act 1980 (LA) [2] . It was ratified due to widespread opinion that it was contrary to public policy for persons to be continuously exposed to litigation for wrongful acts [3] . When substantial time had passed following a wrongful act certain matters may become subject to change such as witnesses’ recollections fading, documentary evidence available to properly judge the case unlikely to be available or in certain cases even exist [4] . These factors could avoid proper justice being served [5] . There are various limitation periods for different types of actions and if limitation has passed, the claim will be bound by statute and the claimant may be prohibited from bringing a claim against the purported wrongdoer [6] . If a claim is brought out of time, the defendant will be able to plead limitation as a defence and the claimant will have the burden of proving that the cause of action arose within the relevant statutory timeframe [7] . Time starts from different dates conditional upon the cause of action, see below. The commencement of proceedings (i.e. when the claim form is received at Court) stops time running for that cause of action [8] . perturbation.justification: The altered text moves the clause concerning the indefinite continuation of obligations to the 'Notices' section, which obscures its importance and clarity. The Law Explanation argues that this placement attempts to circumvent the usual statutory limitations, especially if it makes the term inconspicuous. Scraped Law Snippet 2 indicates that limitation periods are enacted by statute. The perturbation explanation indicates the move to the notices section is an attempt to hide important terms. Placing such a critical term in an unexpected section weakens its prominence and clarity. This makes the contract contradictory as it diminishes a term's prominence, potentially undermining the legal enforceability of the unlimited time obligation.
